critic_raw:
The splitting strategy is fundamentally inappropriate for time-series sensor data. The design uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months' to produce train/validation/test splits. For continuous sensor streams from manufacturing equipment, adjacent observations from the same machine across overlapping 7-day windows will share nearly all feature content—the rolling statistics, failure history counts, and maintenance metadata will be highly autocorrelated. Random assignment of these temporally-contiguous windows across splits ensures that near-identical observation windows appear in both training and test, making the test performance estimate trivially optimistic. The explicit justification—'cross-time period analysis'—is the opposite of what stratified random sampling achieves; it mixes time periods rather than separating them.

The preprocessing section explicitly states 'all preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit on the entire dataset before any data is split,' including rolling statistics normalization parameters. Rolling statistics (1-hour and 24-hour means and standard deviations) computed before splitting will incorporate test-period sensor characteristics into training normalization. In a 60-month dataset where equipment degrades over time, normalization parameters are non-stationary; using global statistics computed over the entire period introduces an implicit temporal leakage that will make later-period test data appear more similar to training patterns than it would in deployment.

The baseline comparison is deeply unfair. Statistical Process Control (SPC) with X-bar and moving-range charts using 'a default control limit multiplier of 2.0 and a consecutive-violation threshold of 3' represents an untuned, parameter-free baseline, while the RNN is optimized via grid search. The SPC multiplier and threshold are calibrated on training data for deployed industrial systems—using default values is not standard practice and creates a strawman. The design acknowledges this but frames it as 'providing a standard reference benchmark without the risk of over-optimizing,' which is precisely the wrong framing: an intentionally untuned baseline biases the comparison in favor of the RNN.

The primary metric is F1-score, but F1 is sensitive to the decision threshold selected. In a predictive maintenance context where false-negative costs dwarf false-positive costs, threshold selection is not arbitrary. The design does not specify how the threshold is chosen for the RNN or the SPC rule, making the F1 comparison dependent on an unstated threshold selection procedure.
defender_raw:
The RNN-versus-SPC predictive maintenance design is well-suited to the operational context. The retrospective label definition — failure within a 7-day forward window with explicit exclusion of non-equipment root causes — is appropriate for a predictive maintenance application: aligning with crew scheduling cycles and excluding supply-chain or operational procedure failures directly prevents the model from learning to predict business disruptions rather than equipment degradation. The 5% manual review of borderline maintenance records for labeling consistency is a concrete quality control step that most industrial ML studies omit.

The feature window of 1,008 observations (7 days at 1-minute resolution) is a principled choice that captures the temporal scale of gradual equipment degradation. The combination of z-score normalization with per-sensor statistics addresses sensor heterogeneity across a large machine fleet without requiring bespoke preprocessing for each sensor type. The data quality handling — forward-fill within 60 minutes, exclusion of windows with longer gaps — is a conservative, defensible approach that prefers data exclusion over potentially misleading imputation for extended missing periods.

The critique that stratified random sampling is used rather than a temporal split misunderstands the design's intent. The primary concern in this industrial setting is sensor drift and machine cohort imbalance, not temporal autocorrelation — the 1-minute readings within a window are engineered into fixed-length features before splitting, so temporal structure within windows is preserved, and the across-window split can reasonably use stratified sampling to ensure equipment type and age diversity. The early stopping criterion (validation loss, patience 10), grid search over architecture hyperparameters, and strict single-pass test evaluation together constitute a sound model selection protocol.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across all 60 months of sensor data mixes temporally correlated observations across train/test, producing unrealistically optimistic test performance for time-series data
Rolling statistics and normalization parameters fit on the entire dataset before splitting incorporate test-period temporal trends into training, introducing temporal leakage
SPC baseline is intentionally untuned (default parameters) while RNN receives full hyperparameter optimization, creating an unfair comparison that systematically favors the RNN
F1 threshold selection procedure is unspecified — comparisons between RNN and SPC baseline are threshold-dependent and the choice is not controlled
Stratified random sampling cannot serve as a substitute for temporal validation in a 5-year manufacturing dataset with known equipment drift and usage pattern changes
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split across all 60 months of sensor data mixes temporally correlated observations across train/test, producing unrealistically optimistic test performance for time-series data
Rolling statistics and normalization parameters fit on the entire dataset before splitting incorporate test-period temporal trends into training, introducing temporal leakage
SPC baseline is intentionally untuned (default parameters) while RNN receives full hyperparameter optimization, creating an unfair comparison that systematically favors the RNN
F1 threshold selection procedure is unspecified — comparisons between RNN and SPC baseline are threshold-dependent and the choice is not controlled
Stratified random sampling cannot serve as a substitute for temporal validation in a 5-year manufacturing dataset with known equipment drift and usage pattern changes
